stimulus: A marine biologist argues that the coral reef off the coast of Meridian Island has been damaged primarily by rising water temperatures rather than by the fishing practices of local communities. She notes that the reef sections most distant from the fishing villages show just as much coral bleaching as the sections nearest to those villages.
stem: Which one of the following is an assumption required by the marine biologist's argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Coral bleaching is the only indicator of damage to a coral reef ecosystem.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The fishing communities near Meridian Island have not recently adopted more sustainable fishing practices.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The reef sections most distant from the fishing villages are not significantly less exposed to the effects of rising water temperatures than the sections nearest to those villages.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rising water temperatures in the region surrounding Meridian Island are caused by global climate change rather than by local factors.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The local fishing communities have been operating in the area for at least as long as coral bleaching has been observed on the reef.

explanation: C is required because the biologist's argument depends on the equal bleaching across reef sections serving as evidence against fishing practices — but this comparison only works if distant sections face similar temperature exposure; if distant sections were cooler, equal bleaching would undermine rather than support her conclusion. A is wrong because the argument focuses specifically on bleaching without needing to exclude other damage indicators. B is wrong because past fishing practices, not recent changes, are at issue. D is wrong because the cause of rising temperatures is irrelevant to whether temperatures (vs. fishing) caused the bleaching. E is wrong because the duration of fishing activity is not needed to establish the argument's conclusion.

stimulus: A marine biologist argues that the coral reef off the coast of Meridian Island will recover from recent bleaching damage within five years. Her argument rests on the observation that water temperatures in the region have returned to historically normal levels, and that coral reefs always recover once the stressful conditions that caused bleaching are eliminated.
stem: Which one of the following is an assumption required by the marine biologist's argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Water temperature is the only factor that affects the long-term health of coral reefs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Elevated water temperature was the cause of the bleaching damage to the Meridian Island reef.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The Meridian Island reef has experienced bleaching events in the past and recovered from them.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Coral reefs that recover from bleaching are more resistant to future bleaching events.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Marine biologists have been monitoring the Meridian Island reef for at least five years.

explanation: The argument concludes that the reef will recover because stressful conditions have been eliminated, but this reasoning only works if elevated water temperature was actually what caused the bleaching — if some other stressor caused it and remains present, the conclusion fails; B is therefore a necessary bridging assumption. A is too strong and not required since the argument only invokes temperature as the relevant stressor in this case. C is irrelevant because the general principle in the argument does not depend on this reef's particular history. D introduces a claim about future resilience that the argument never relies upon. E is irrelevant to whether the reef will recover.

stimulus: Marine biologists have discovered that coral reefs in the region near Talova Island have begun recovering after decades of decline. The recovery is occurring because water temperatures in the region have dropped to levels that allow coral to thrive. However, a proposed offshore drilling operation near Talova Island would halt this recovery, since ______.
stem: The conclusion of the argument is properly drawn if which one of the following completes the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: offshore drilling operations are typically opposed by environmental groups

choices.label: B
choices.text: the equipment used in offshore drilling operations raises water temperatures in surrounding areas to levels at which coral cannot thrive

choices.label: C
choices.text: coral reefs provide habitat for a large number of marine species

choices.label: D
choices.text: the proposed drilling operation has not yet received approval from the relevant regulatory agencies

choices.label: E
choices.text: coral reef recovery has been observed in other regions where water temperatures have similarly declined

explanation: B is correct because it directly supplies the missing causal link needed to conclude that the drilling would halt coral recovery—namely, that drilling raises water temperatures to levels harmful to coral, which is precisely the condition undermining the recovery; A is wrong because opposition from environmental groups is irrelevant to whether drilling would actually halt recovery; C is wrong because the ecological importance of coral reefs does not establish that drilling would harm them; D is wrong because the regulatory status of the operation does not bear on whether it would halt coral recovery if approved; and E is wrong because observations from other regions merely confirm the temperature-coral relationship already stated in the argument without bridging the gap between drilling and temperature increase.

stimulus: Marine biologists have found that the coral reefs surrounding Kelton Island have begun to recover after years of bleaching damage. This recovery is occurring because water temperatures in the area have dropped to levels that support coral growth. However, the reefs will not fully recover unless the population of parrotfish—which remove algae that would otherwise smother the coral—remains robust. The local fishing cooperative recently announced plans to significantly increase its harvest of parrotfish. So the coral reefs surrounding Kelton Island will not fully recover, since ______.
stem: The conclusion of the argument is properly drawn if which one of the following completes the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the fishing cooperative's increased harvesting will take place before the reefs have had a chance to fully recover

choices.label: B
choices.text: increased harvesting of parrotfish by the cooperative will reduce the parrotfish population to a level that is not robust

choices.label: C
choices.text: coral reefs in other regions have also suffered from declining parrotfish populations

choices.label: D
choices.text: the fishing cooperative has harvested parrotfish in the area for several decades

choices.label: E
choices.text: water temperatures surrounding Kelton Island may rise again in future years

explanation: B is correct because it directly supplies the missing link between the cooperative's increased harvest and the established condition for reef failure (non-robust parrotfish population), making the conclusion logically follow; A introduces a timing consideration that doesn't guarantee the conclusion; C is irrelevant to Kelton Island's specific situation; D establishes historical fishing but says nothing about whether future increases will harm the population; and E introduces a separate threat (temperature) that is already addressed as resolved and does not connect to the parrotfish condition.

stimulus: A nutritional researcher argues that the decline in cognitive performance observed in elderly residents of Maplewood Assisted Living Center over the past three years is caused by a deficiency in omega-3 fatty acids. The researcher notes that the center switched to a new meal provider three years ago, and that the new provider's menu contains significantly less fish and other omega-3-rich foods than the previous menu did. Blood tests confirm that the residents' average omega-3 levels are currently well below the recommended threshold.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the researcher's argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Residents who have been taking omega-3 supplements since the menu change show cognitive performance levels comparable to those measured before the switch to the new meal provider.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The new meal provider serves residents at several other assisted living centers in the region.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cognitive decline is commonly observed in elderly populations regardless of their dietary habits.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The residents of Maplewood Assisted Living Center are, on average, three years older than they were when the meal provider changed.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Omega-3 fatty acids are found in sources other than fish, including certain nuts and plant-based oils.

explanation: A strengthens the argument by providing evidence that maintaining omega-3 levels (via supplements) prevented the cognitive decline seen in other residents, directly supporting the causal link between omega-3 deficiency and cognitive decline; B is irrelevant because it says nothing about outcomes at other centers; C weakens the argument by suggesting the decline may be independent of diet; D introduces an alternative explanation (aging) that actually undermines the researcher's causal claim; and E is irrelevant because it neither confirms nor denies that the residents are deficient due to the menu change.

stimulus: Researchers studying urban heat islands have proposed that planting additional trees along city streets would significantly reduce summer temperatures in densely populated neighborhoods. Their proposal is based on the observation that shaded surfaces absorb less solar radiation than unshaded surfaces, and that trees release water vapor through transpiration, which cools the surrounding air. Critics have questioned whether street trees could have a meaningful effect, given that most urban heat is generated by vehicles and industrial activity rather than direct solar absorption.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the researchers' proposal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cities that have recently expanded their urban forestry programs have reported measurable reductions in peak summer temperatures in the neighborhoods where trees were planted, independent of changes in vehicle traffic levels.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Trees planted along streets in temperate climates require significantly less maintenance than trees planted in arid urban environments.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The cost of planting and maintaining street trees is substantially lower than the cost of installing mechanical cooling systems in public spaces.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Residents of neighborhoods with more green space report higher levels of satisfaction with their living conditions than residents of neighborhoods with less green space.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Some municipalities have begun replacing aging street trees with species that are more resistant to drought and urban pollution.

explanation: A directly strengthens the researchers' proposal by providing real-world evidence that urban tree planting reduces summer temperatures even when vehicle traffic—the critics' alternative explanation—remains unchanged, thus addressing the gap between the proposed mechanism and the critics' objection; B is irrelevant because maintenance requirements have no bearing on cooling effectiveness; C addresses cost-effectiveness rather than whether trees reduce temperatures; D concerns resident satisfaction, not temperature reduction; and E is about tree species replacement, which does not support or undermine the cooling effect claim.

stimulus: A city council is debating whether to install additional streetlights in the Millbrook neighborhood. Proponents argue that the installation will reduce crime in the area. They point to data showing that over the past two years, neighborhoods in the city that received new streetlights experienced, on average, a 30 percent decrease in reported nighttime crimes compared to a 5 percent decrease citywide. Therefore, installing streetlights in Millbrook will likely reduce crime there as well.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Some crimes, such as vandalism and theft, occur more frequently during daytime hours than at night.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The neighborhoods that received new streetlights were selected for the program precisely because city officials had already identified them as areas with rapidly declining crime rates.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Streetlight installation projects typically take between three and six months to complete.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Millbrook residents have repeatedly requested better street lighting for reasons related to pedestrian safety rather than crime prevention.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The cost of installing and maintaining additional streetlights in Millbrook would exceed the city's current budget allocation for neighborhood improvement projects.

explanation: B weakens the argument by providing an alternative explanation for the crime reduction in lit neighborhoods — the crime was already declining before the lights were installed, meaning the lights may not deserve credit — while A is irrelevant because the argument concerns nighttime crime specifically, C is irrelevant because it addresses timing of installation rather than effectiveness, D is irrelevant because residents' motivations don't undermine whether lighting reduces crime, and E raises a budgetary concern that is outside the scope of whether lighting would actually reduce crime.

stimulus: A city transportation authority recently installed automated fare-collection kiosks at all subway stations to replace human ticket agents. Since the installation, average passenger wait times at fare gates have dropped by 40 percent. The authority concluded that the kiosks are responsible for the improvement in wait times, and announced plans to install similar kiosks in bus terminals citywide.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the transportation authority's conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The automated kiosks require periodic maintenance that temporarily takes them out of service.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The kiosks were installed during the same period in which the city introduced an app allowing passengers to purchase and store fares on their smartphones before arriving at stations.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Some passengers report finding the kiosk interfaces less intuitive than interacting with a human ticket agent.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Human ticket agents were capable of handling complex ticketing requests that the automated kiosks cannot process.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The transportation authority conducted passenger satisfaction surveys both before and after the kiosk installation.

explanation: B weakens the argument by offering an alternative explanation for the reduction in wait times — the smartphone app, not the kiosks, may be responsible — while A is irrelevant to whether kiosks caused the improvement, C addresses user preference rather than wait times, D actually supports retaining human agents but doesn't undermine the causal claim about wait times, and E is irrelevant because conducting surveys neither supports nor undermines the conclusion about what caused the wait time reduction.

stimulus: Nutritionist: Studies show that people who eat breakfast regularly tend to have lower rates of obesity than those who skip breakfast. Therefore, skipping breakfast must cause people to gain excess weight. To reduce obesity rates in this country, we should launch a public health campaign encouraging everyone to eat breakfast every morning.
stem: Which one of the following most accurately describes a flaw in the nutritionist's reasoning?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The argument draws a conclusion about an entire population based on evidence drawn from an unrepresentative sample.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The argument treats a correlation between two phenomena as proof that one phenomenon causes the other.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The argument dismisses a competing hypothesis without providing any evidence against it.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The argument assumes that what is beneficial for some individuals must be beneficial for all individuals.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The argument confuses a necessary condition for reducing obesity with a sufficient condition for reducing obesity.

explanation: B is correct because the nutritionist observes a correlation (breakfast-eaters have lower obesity rates) and incorrectly infers causation (skipping breakfast causes obesity), ignoring alternative explanations such as the possibility that other healthy habits common among breakfast-eaters are the actual cause; A is wrong because the argument does not discuss the representativeness of its sample; C is wrong because no competing hypothesis is raised and then dismissed; D is wrong because the argument makes a universal recommendation but does not explicitly argue from individual benefit to collective benefit; E is wrong because the argument never discusses necessary versus sufficient conditions for reducing obesity.

stimulus: Nutritionist: Studies show that people who eat breakfast regularly tend to have lower body weight than people who skip breakfast. Therefore, if overweight people want to lose weight, they should start eating breakfast every day, since doing so will cause them to weigh less.
stem: Which one of the following most accurately describes a flaw in the nutritionist's reasoning?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The argument draws a conclusion about all overweight people based on studies that may have examined only a small, unrepresentative sample.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The argument treats a correlation between two phenomena as though it establishes that one of those phenomena causes the other.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The argument recommends a course of action without considering whether overweight people are capable of following it.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The argument mistakes a condition that is sufficient for weight loss for one that is necessary for weight loss.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The argument applies a finding about individual behavior to draw a conclusion about population-level trends.

explanation: B is correct because the nutritionist observes a correlation (breakfast eaters weigh less) and incorrectly infers a causal direction (eating breakfast causes lower weight), ignoring that the relationship could be explained by other factors or reversed causation; A is wrong because the argument's flaw is not about sample size but about inferring causation from correlation; C is wrong because the argument makes no assumptions about capability; D is wrong because the argument never treats breakfast eating as necessary for weight loss, only recommends it; E is wrong because the argument moves from population data to individual advice, which is the opposite direction of part-to-whole reasoning.

stimulus: Neuroscientists have established that the hippocampus plays a central role in the formation of long-term memories. Patients with severe hippocampal damage are typically unable to form new long-term memories, though they retain memories formed before the damage occurred. Interestingly, such patients can still acquire new motor skills, such as learning to ride a bicycle, even though they have no conscious recollection of having practiced those skills.
stem: Which one of the following is most strongly supported by the information above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The hippocampus is involved in retaining memories formed before any damage to it has occurred.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The acquisition of motor skills does not depend entirely on the same memory systems that the hippocampus supports.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patients with hippocampal damage experience no deficits in any aspect of learning or cognition.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Conscious recollection of past events is necessary for the development of any practical skill.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The hippocampus is the only brain structure involved in the formation of memories of any kind.

explanation: B is correct because the stimulus directly states that hippocampal damage prevents new long-term memory formation yet leaves motor skill acquisition intact, which supports the inference that motor skill learning relies on different memory systems than those the hippocampus supports; A is contradicted by the stimulus, which says patients cannot form new long-term memories but retain old ones — storage and retrieval of old memories need not involve the hippocampus; C is clearly wrong since the stimulus explicitly describes a significant deficit (inability to form new long-term memories); D is contradicted by the fact that patients acquire motor skills without conscious recollection of practicing them; and E is undermined by the evidence that motor skill memories form independently of the hippocampus.

stimulus: Every archaeological site that has yielded fossilized pollen samples has provided researchers with data about the vegetation patterns of ancient climates. Some archaeological sites in the Indus Valley have not yielded fossilized pollen samples, but all of those sites have produced ceramic artifacts. No site that has produced ceramic artifacts has failed to advance our understanding of ancient trade networks.
stem: Which one of the following is most strongly supported by the information above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Every archaeological site in the Indus Valley has advanced our understanding of ancient trade networks.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Some sites that have provided data about ancient vegetation patterns have also advanced our understanding of ancient trade networks.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The Indus Valley sites that have not yielded fossilized pollen samples have advanced our understanding of ancient trade networks.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fossilized pollen samples are more valuable to researchers than ceramic artifacts.

choices.label: E
choices.text: All archaeological sites that produce ceramic artifacts are located in the Indus Valley.

explanation: C is correct because the stimulus directly states that some Indus Valley sites have not yielded pollen samples but have produced ceramic artifacts, and that no ceramic-artifact site has failed to advance understanding of trade networks — meaning those pollen-lacking Indus Valley sites must have advanced trade network understanding; A is wrong because the stimulus only speaks to sites lacking pollen samples, not all Indus Valley sites; B introduces an unsupported connection between pollen-sample sites and trade network understanding; D makes a value judgment not supported by the factual claims; and E reverses the relationship, incorrectly claiming all ceramic-artifact sites are in the Indus Valley.

stimulus: Every financial advisor who is licensed in the state of Merova is required to disclose all potential conflicts of interest to their clients. Any financial advisor who fails to disclose a conflict of interest is subject to disciplinary review by the Merova Financial Regulatory Board. No advisor currently under disciplinary review is permitted to manage client accounts independently.
stem: If the statements above are true, which one of the following must be true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Every financial advisor who manages client accounts independently has disclosed all potential conflicts of interest.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Some financial advisors licensed in Merova have been subject to disciplinary review.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Any financial advisor licensed in Merova who fails to disclose a conflict of interest cannot independently manage client accounts.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Every advisor subject to disciplinary review by the Merova Financial Regulatory Board is licensed in Merova.

choices.label: E
choices.text: No financial advisor who manages client accounts independently has ever had a conflict of interest.

explanation: C must be true because the chain of conditionals establishes that a Merova-licensed advisor who fails to disclose a conflict of interest is subject to disciplinary review, and any advisor under disciplinary review cannot manage accounts independently — so a licensed advisor who fails to disclose cannot independently manage accounts. A goes too far because the contrapositive only guarantees that independent account managers are not currently under disciplinary review, not that they have disclosed all conflicts. B is not guaranteed because the stimulus does not establish that any advisor has actually failed to disclose a conflict. D reverses the direction of the first conditional, which runs from licensed to required to disclose, not from disciplinary review back to licensure. E confuses having a conflict of interest with failing to disclose one — an advisor can have a conflict while still properly disclosing it.

stimulus: All financial advisors licensed by the National Investment Board are required to disclose any conflicts of interest to their clients. No one who fails to disclose conflicts of interest is permitted to manage retirement accounts. Thornfield Wealth Management employs only financial advisors who manage retirement accounts.
stem: If the statements above are true, which one of the following must be true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: All financial advisors who disclose conflicts of interest are licensed by the National Investment Board.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Every financial advisor at Thornfield Wealth Management is licensed by the National Investment Board.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Every financial advisor at Thornfield Wealth Management discloses conflicts of interest to their clients.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No financial advisor at Thornfield Wealth Management has ever had a conflict of interest.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Some financial advisors licensed by the National Investment Board manage retirement accounts at Thornfield Wealth Management.

explanation: C must be true because: all Thornfield advisors manage retirement accounts → all Thornfield advisors are permitted to manage retirement accounts → none of them failed to disclose conflicts of interest → all of them disclose conflicts of interest to their clients. A incorrectly reverses the first conditional (disclosure does not entail licensure). B is wrong because Thornfield advisors must disclose conflicts of interest, but this requirement could arise from the retirement account rule rather than from National Investment Board licensure — they need not be licensed by that board. D goes too far, as having conflicts of interest is different from failing to disclose them. E is unsupported because Thornfield advisors need not be licensed by the National Investment Board to satisfy the disclosure requirement.

stimulus: A city arts council has established the following policies and facts regarding its annual grant program: Every artist who receives a grant from the council must be either a resident of the city or a member of an accredited arts organization, but not both. All city residents who receive grants are required to submit a portfolio of at least ten works. The sculptor Navarro, who received a grant this year, submitted a portfolio of exactly six works. Furthermore, every member of an accredited arts organization who receives a grant must attend at least one council-sponsored workshop.
stem: If the statements above are all true, which one of the following CANNOT be true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Navarro attended at least one council-sponsored workshop this year.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Some city residents who received grants this year submitted portfolios of more than ten works.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Navarro is a resident of the city.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Some members of accredited arts organizations who received grants this year are not city residents.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Every grant recipient this year who is a member of an accredited arts organization attended more than one council-sponsored workshop.

explanation: C cannot be true because if Navarro were a city resident, the policy would require a portfolio of at least ten works, yet Navarro submitted only six works, directly contradicting that requirement; A could be true because Navarro, as a member of an accredited arts organization (the only remaining option), is required to attend at least one workshop and may have done so; B could be true since the stimulus sets a minimum of ten works for city residents but places no upper limit; D could be true since the stimulus states grant recipients are either residents or organization members but not both, so organization members would by definition not be city residents; and E could be true since the stimulus requires at least one workshop attendance but does not prohibit more than one.

stimulus: A city's transit authority has compiled the following facts about its bus routes: Every express bus route in the city makes fewer than 10 stops. All bus routes that serve the downtown financial district make at least 15 stops. The Route 7 bus is classified as an express bus route. Every bus route in the city that makes fewer than 10 stops charges passengers a flat fare of exactly $2.50.
stem: If the statements above are all true, which one of the following CANNOT be true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Some bus routes that are not express routes charge a flat fare of $2.50.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Route 7 serves the downtown financial district.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Some express bus routes make fewer than 5 stops.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There are bus routes in the city that make more than 20 stops.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Some bus routes that serve the downtown financial district do not charge a flat fare of $2.50.

explanation: B cannot be true because Route 7 is an express bus route, so it makes fewer than 10 stops, yet any route serving the downtown financial district must make at least 15 stops — these two conditions are mutually exclusive, making it impossible for Route 7 to serve the downtown financial district; A could be true since the flat fare rule applies to any route with fewer than 10 stops, regardless of express classification; C could be true since the stimulus only sets an upper bound of fewer than 10 stops for express routes, not a lower bound; D could be true since the stimulus places no ceiling on the number of stops for non-express routes; and E could be true since downtown financial district routes make at least 15 stops and thus are not required to charge the $2.50 flat fare, which applies only to routes with fewer than 10 stops.

stimulus: A major metropolitan library system recently replaced its entire collection of physical encyclopedias and reference books with digital equivalents accessible on in-library computer terminals. Following this change, librarians reported a dramatic increase in the number of patrons visiting the library each week. Yet, despite the surge in foot traffic, the total number of reference questions that librarians answered per week fell sharply after the transition.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to explain the apparent discrepancy described above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The digital reference materials are updated more frequently than the physical books they replaced, making them more accurate and useful to researchers.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Many of the additional patrons who began visiting the library after the transition came primarily to use the computer terminals for tasks such as job searches and email, and found they could answer their own informational queries without librarian assistance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The library system spent a considerable portion of its annual budget on the transition to digital reference materials, leaving fewer funds for staffing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Several nearby library branches closed in the months following the transition, redirecting their patrons to the main library location.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Librarians at the metropolitan library system received training on the new digital reference systems before they were made available to the public.

explanation: B resolves the paradox by explaining that the new visitors came to use computers for non-research purposes and could self-serve any informational needs, reconciling increased foot traffic with fewer librarian-answered questions; A is irrelevant to the paradox since accuracy of materials doesn't explain the drop in reference questions; C could reduce librarian availability but doesn't explain why more visitors would ask fewer questions overall; D explains increased foot traffic but worsens rather than resolves the paradox since relocated patrons would presumably still ask reference questions; E is irrelevant because librarian training has no bearing on how many questions patrons choose to ask.

stimulus: A study of urban commuters found that residents of Millford who switched from driving personal vehicles to riding the city's newly expanded subway system saved an average of 45 minutes per day on their commutes. Despite this dramatic time savings, follow-up surveys conducted six months after the subway expansion revealed that overall satisfaction with daily commuting among Millford residents had declined compared to the period before the expansion.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent paradox described above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The cost of monthly subway passes in Millford increased by 20 percent in the six months following the expansion.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Many Millford residents who previously drove to work were unaware of the subway expansion until several months after it occurred.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The subway expansion caused significant road construction that dramatically worsened traffic for the majority of residents who continued to drive, and most Millford residents still commute by car.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Subway ridership in Millford increased by 35 percent in the six months following the expansion of the system.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Studies in other cities have shown that commuters generally adapt quickly to new transit options and soon take reduced travel times for granted.

explanation: C resolves the paradox by explaining that while subway riders saved time, the majority of commuters still drove and suffered worsened traffic from construction related to the expansion, causing overall commuter satisfaction to fall despite the time savings for the minority who switched; A might reduce satisfaction but only for subway riders and doesn't explain why overall satisfaction declined given the time savings; B is irrelevant to satisfaction levels since it concerns awareness rather than experience; D deepens rather than resolves the paradox by confirming more people benefited from the faster commute; and E, if anything, worsens the paradox by suggesting commuters should have adapted positively to the time savings.

stimulus: If a musician practices fewer than three hours per day, her technique will deteriorate. If her technique deteriorates, she will either lose performance opportunities or receive negative reviews. Therefore, if she does not receive negative reviews, either she practices at least three hours per day or she will lose performance opportunities.
stem: Which one of the following arguments is most closely parallel in its reasoning to the reasoning in the argument above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: If an athlete does not maintain a proper diet, his performance will decline. If his performance declines, he will either lose his starting position or fail to meet training targets. Therefore, if he does not lose his starting position, either he maintains a proper diet or he will fail to meet training targets.

choices.label: B
choices.text: If a student does not study regularly, she will perform poorly on exams. If she performs poorly on exams, her scholarship will be revoked. Therefore, if the student studies regularly, her scholarship will not be revoked.

choices.label: C
choices.text: If a chef does not use fresh ingredients, the dish will taste bland. The dish will be either returned or given a poor rating if it tastes bland. Therefore, since this dish was not returned, the chef used fresh ingredients.

choices.label: D
choices.text: If a pilot fails the certification test, she will not be allowed to fly commercially. If she is not allowed to fly commercially, she will either seek recertification or change careers. Therefore, if she does not change careers, she either passed the certification test or she will seek recertification.

choices.label: E
choices.text: If a surgeon does not complete continuing education, his license may be suspended. If either his license is suspended or his malpractice insurance lapses, he cannot practice. Therefore, if the surgeon cannot practice, he failed to complete continuing education.

explanation: The stimulus has the structure: (not-P → Q), (Q → R or S), therefore (not-S → P or R); choice D matches exactly with (not-P → Q), (Q → R or S), therefore (not-S → P or R); choice A is superficially similar but the conclusion's negated element ('does not lose his starting position') corresponds to R rather than S, breaking the parallel; choice B has only two consequences and a simple chain, missing the disjunctive conclusion; choice C uses a categorical premise about a specific dish rather than a conditional structure and draws a definitive rather than conditional conclusion; choice E has a different structure where the conclusion attributes causation rather than presenting a conditional disjunction.

stimulus: If a software update is released without thorough testing, it will contain critical bugs. If a software update contains critical bugs, it will either crash user devices or corrupt stored data. Therefore, if a software update does not corrupt stored data, then either it was released after thorough testing or it will crash user devices.
stem: Which one of the following arguments is most closely parallel in its reasoning to the reasoning in the argument above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: If a bridge is built without proper inspections, it will have structural flaws. If a bridge has structural flaws, it will either collapse under load or require costly repairs. Therefore, if a bridge does not require costly repairs, then either it was built with proper inspections or it will collapse under load.

choices.label: B
choices.text: If a recipe is prepared without fresh ingredients, the dish will taste bland. If the dish tastes bland, diners will either send it back or leave poor reviews. Therefore, if diners do not send the dish back and do not leave poor reviews, the recipe was prepared with fresh ingredients.

choices.label: C
choices.text: If a vaccine is stored at the wrong temperature, it will lose potency. A vaccine that loses potency will fail to prevent illness. Therefore, if a vaccine is stored at the correct temperature, it will prevent illness.

choices.label: D
choices.text: If a contract is signed without legal review, it may be unenforceable. If a contract is unenforceable, one party will either suffer financial loss or seek arbitration. Therefore, if one party seeks arbitration, the contract was signed without legal review.

choices.label: E
choices.text: If a ship is launched without sea trials, it will have navigational problems. If a ship has navigational problems, it will either run aground or require a tugboat escort. Therefore, if a ship both runs aground and requires a tugboat escort, it was launched without sea trials.

explanation: The stimulus has the structure: P→Q, Q→(R or S), therefore ¬S→(¬P or R). Answer A matches exactly: building without inspections→structural flaws, structural flaws→(collapse or costly repairs), therefore ¬costly repairs→(proper inspections or collapse). Answer B draws a conclusion with two negated conditions yielding the original premise's negation, a different structure. Answer C chains two conditionals into a simple hypothetical syllogism without a disjunctive consequent. Answer D commits an affirming-the-consequent fallacy in its conclusion rather than producing a disjunctive conditional. Answer E concludes that both disjuncts being true implies the original antecedent, a structurally different pattern.

stimulus: Every scientist who attended the climate conference had published at least one peer-reviewed paper on atmospheric modeling. Dr. Okafor has published several peer-reviewed papers on atmospheric modeling. Therefore, Dr. Okafor must have attended the climate conference.
stem: Which one of the following contains flawed reasoning most similar to the flawed reasoning in the argument above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Every chef who competed in the regional tournament had trained at a culinary institute. Marcus trained at a culinary institute. Therefore, Marcus must have competed in the regional tournament.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Every chef who competed in the regional tournament had trained at a culinary institute. Marcus did not train at a culinary institute. Therefore, Marcus did not compete in the regional tournament.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Some chefs who trained at culinary institutes have competed in regional tournaments. Marcus trained at a culinary institute. Therefore, Marcus has probably competed in a regional tournament.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Every chef who competed in the regional tournament had trained at a culinary institute. Marcus competed in the regional tournament. Therefore, Marcus must have trained at a culinary institute.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Every chef who trained at a culinary institute and competed in the regional tournament won an award. Marcus trained at a culinary institute and competed in the regional tournament. Therefore, Marcus won an award.

explanation: The stimulus commits the fallacy of affirming the consequent: from 'All A → B' (attended conference → published papers) and 'B is true' (Okafor published papers), it wrongly concludes 'A is true' (Okafor attended). Choice A matches exactly—from 'All who competed → trained at institute' and 'Marcus trained at institute,' it wrongly concludes Marcus competed. Choice B commits the different flaw of denying the antecedent. Choice C uses 'some' instead of 'all,' making it a different kind of probabilistic error. Choice D correctly applies the stated conditional via modus ponens and is therefore a valid argument. Choice E also correctly applies a conjunction of conditions to reach a valid conclusion with no flaw.

stimulus: All of the athletes who qualified for the national championship had trained at high altitude for at least six weeks. Serena trained at high altitude for at least six weeks. Therefore, Serena must have qualified for the national championship.
stem: Which one of the following contains flawed reasoning most similar to the flawed reasoning in the argument above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: All of the manuscripts accepted for publication had been reviewed by at least two editors. This manuscript was not reviewed by at least two editors. Therefore, this manuscript was not accepted for publication.

choices.label: B
choices.text: All of the bridges that passed the safety inspection were built with reinforced steel. The Harmon Bridge was built with reinforced steel. Therefore, the Harmon Bridge must have passed the safety inspection.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Some of the investors who profited from the merger had received advance notice of the deal. Marcus received advance notice of the deal. Therefore, Marcus must have profited from the merger.

choices.label: D
choices.text: All of the wines awarded a gold medal at the festival had been aged for at least five years. This wine was awarded a gold medal at the festival. Therefore, this wine was aged for at least five years.

choices.label: E
choices.text: All of the candidates who advanced to the final round had submitted a portfolio. Yolanda did not submit a portfolio. Therefore, Yolanda did not advance to the final round.

explanation: The stimulus commits affirming the consequent: from 'All A → B' and 'B is true of X,' it wrongly concludes 'A is true of X' (all qualifiers trained at altitude; Serena trained at altitude; therefore Serena qualified). Choice B matches exactly—from 'All bridges passing inspection → built with reinforced steel' and 'Harmon Bridge built with reinforced steel,' it wrongly concludes the bridge passed inspection. Choice A commits denying the antecedent (not reviewed → not accepted), a different flaw. Choice C uses 'some' rather than 'all,' making the inference from a weaker premise and representing a different error. Choice D correctly applies modus ponens (gold medal → aged five years; this wine won gold; therefore aged five years), a valid argument. Choice E correctly applies modus tollens (all who advanced submitted a portfolio; Yolanda didn't submit; therefore she didn't advance), also a valid inference.

stimulus:
Ramirez: Electric vehicles should not be considered an environmentally friendly transportation option. Although they produce no tailpipe emissions, the electricity used to charge them is predominantly generated by burning fossil fuels, meaning their overall carbon footprint is comparable to that of conventional gasoline-powered cars.

Sung: Electric vehicles genuinely are an environmentally friendly option. Even accounting for the carbon emissions associated with electricity generation, studies consistently show that electric vehicles produce significantly fewer greenhouse gas emissions over their full lifecycle than gasoline-powered vehicles do.
stem: On the basis of their statements, Ramirez and Sung are committed to disagreeing over which one of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Electric vehicles produce no emissions directly from their engines while being driven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The environmental impact of a vehicle should be assessed over its full lifecycle rather than only at the point of use.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A significant portion of the electricity used to charge electric vehicles comes from fossil fuel sources.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Electric vehicles result in substantially lower total greenhouse gas emissions than gasoline-powered vehicles when electricity generation is taken into account.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Reducing greenhouse gas emissions is an important goal of environmentally friendly transportation policy.

explanation: D is correct because Ramirez explicitly claims that accounting for electricity generation makes electric vehicles' carbon footprint comparable to gasoline cars (denying D), while Sung explicitly asserts that even accounting for electricity generation, electric vehicles produce significantly fewer emissions (affirming D); A is a point Ramirez concedes and Sung does not dispute; B is a methodological premise both speakers implicitly accept by considering electricity generation in their analyses; C is a factual claim Ramirez asserts and Sung does not challenge; E is a background value neither speaker disputes.

stimulus:
Reyes: Cities should ban the use of ride-sharing apps during peak commute hours. Traffic studies consistently show that ride-sharing vehicles make up a disproportionate share of downtown congestion during those periods, and reducing that congestion would meaningfully improve air quality for urban residents.

Fontaine: Banning ride-sharing apps during peak commute hours would not reduce downtown congestion. Commuters who currently use ride-sharing services would simply switch to personal vehicles, which would put just as many cars—if not more—on the road during those same hours.
stem: On the basis of their statements, Reyes and Fontaine are committed to disagreeing over which one of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ride-sharing vehicles contribute to downtown traffic congestion during peak commute hours.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reducing downtown traffic congestion would improve air quality for urban residents.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Banning ride-sharing apps during peak commute hours would decrease the total number of vehicles on downtown roads during those hours.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cities have the legal authority to regulate when ride-sharing apps may operate within their boundaries.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Personal vehicles are less fuel-efficient than ride-sharing vehicles on a per-passenger basis.

explanation: C is correct because Reyes implicitly affirms that a ban would reduce congestion (the stated goal of her proposal), while Fontaine explicitly denies this by arguing commuters would simply shift to personal vehicles; A is something Reyes asserts and Fontaine does not dispute, since Fontaine's argument accepts that ride-sharing vehicles currently contribute to congestion; B is a claim Reyes makes that Fontaine never addresses; D is addressed by neither speaker; E is a factual claim about fuel efficiency that neither speaker raises.

stimulus: A large metropolitan school district recently adopted a policy requiring all middle school students to participate in a daily 20-minute free reading period, during which students may read any book of their choosing. District officials point to data showing that standardized reading comprehension scores among middle schoolers rose by an average of 8 percentile points in the year following the policy's implementation. They conclude that the free reading program is responsible for the improvement in students' reading comprehension.
stem: Which of the following would be most useful to know in order to evaluate the district officials' conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether students in the district preferred fiction or nonfiction titles during the free reading period

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whether the district also introduced a new reading comprehension curriculum in classrooms during the same year the free reading program was implemented

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whether the school librarians who selected books for the free reading period received specialized training in adolescent literacy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Whether the standardized tests used to measure reading comprehension were administered at the beginning or end of the school day

choices.label: E
choices.text: Whether middle school students in the district found the free reading period more enjoyable than other structured classroom activities

explanation: B is correct because if a new reading curriculum was introduced simultaneously, it could be the actual cause of improved scores, weakening the officials' conclusion, whereas if no such curriculum change occurred, the free reading program is more plausibly responsible, strengthening it — either way, this information directly tests the causal claim; A is wrong because genre preferences among students do not bear on whether the program caused the score increase; C is wrong because librarians' training credentials are irrelevant to whether the free reading program itself produced the comprehension gains; D is wrong because test scheduling has no bearing on whether the program caused the improvement in scores; and E is wrong because student enjoyment of the program is irrelevant to whether it was causally responsible for the measured gains in reading comprehension.

stimulus: A national coffee chain recently introduced a new loyalty rewards app, replacing its physical punch cards. After six months, the chain's average monthly revenue per store increased by 22%. The chain's marketing director concluded that the app is responsible for this revenue growth, arguing that the app's personalized discount offers encourage customers to visit more frequently and spend more per visit than they did when using punch cards.
stem: Which of the following would be most useful to know in order to evaluate the marketing director's conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether the coffee chain's app was developed by an in-house team or outsourced to a third-party software company

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whether the coffee chain raised its menu prices during the same six-month period in which the app was introduced

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whether the marketing director personally uses the loyalty app when purchasing coffee at the chain

choices.label: D
choices.text: Whether the coffee chain's stores are located primarily in urban areas or suburban areas

choices.label: E
choices.text: Whether the coffee chain plans to introduce additional features to the app in the coming year

explanation: B is correct because if the chain raised menu prices during the same period, the revenue increase could be attributable to higher prices rather than the app's effect on customer behavior, which would weaken the director's conclusion, whereas if prices remained stable, the revenue growth is more plausibly linked to increased customer frequency and spending driven by the app, strengthening the conclusion; A is irrelevant because the app's development origin has no bearing on whether the app caused the revenue increase; C introduces an ad hominem consideration that has no bearing on the causal claim about the app's effect on revenue; D is irrelevant because store location type does not help determine whether the app, rather than some other factor, caused the revenue growth; and E is irrelevant because future planned features have no bearing on whether the app caused the revenue increase that has already been observed.

stimulus: A software company released a new application that had not been fully tested because management wanted to beat a competitor to market. Shortly after release, a security flaw in the untested code allowed hackers to access users' personal financial data. The company's legal team argued that the company could not claim it was an unforeseeable event and therefore could not seek to limit its liability to affected users, since the vulnerability existed precisely because the company had chosen to skip the standard security testing protocols.
stem: The legal team's reasoning best illustrates which one of the following principles?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Companies that rush products to market in order to outpace competitors should be prohibited from releasing software until all testing is complete.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A party cannot invoke the unforeseeable nature of a harmful outcome to reduce its liability when that outcome resulted directly from the party's own decision to forgo precautions designed to prevent it.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Organizations that handle users' financial data are held to a higher standard of care than those that handle only non-sensitive information.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Any business that suffers a security breach caused by hackers bears no responsibility for damages because the hackers are the proximate cause of the harm.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The degree of liability a company faces for a data breach is proportional to the amount of financial harm suffered by the affected users.

explanation: B is correct because the legal team's reasoning hinges specifically on the company having skipped safety precautions designed to catch this very kind of vulnerability, making the harm foreseeable and thus precluding a reduced-liability defense—exactly what B generalizes; A is wrong because the legal team does not argue that rushing to market should be prohibited, only that it affects liability; C is wrong because the sensitivity of the data type plays no role in the legal team's reasoning; D is wrong because it directly contradicts the legal team's conclusion by absolving the company of responsibility; E is wrong because the magnitude of user harm is never invoked in the legal team's argument about the company's liability.

stimulus: A software company released a new application that contained a known security vulnerability the developers had identified during testing but decided not to fix before launch in order to meet the release deadline. When users' personal data was subsequently stolen by hackers exploiting that vulnerability, the company's legal team argued that the company could not avoid liability by claiming the attack was unforeseeable, since the company had already identified the specific flaw that was exploited.
stem: The legal team's reasoning best illustrates which one of the following principles?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A company that releases software containing any defect is automatically liable for all harm that results from use of that software.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Organizations should delay product releases whenever testing reveals unresolved technical problems.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A party cannot disclaim responsibility for harm on the grounds that the harm was unforeseeable when that party had prior knowledge of the specific risk that caused the harm.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hackers who exploit known security vulnerabilities bear greater moral responsibility for data theft than companies that fail to patch those vulnerabilities.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Meeting a product deadline is never a legitimate justification for releasing software that has not undergone complete security testing.

explanation: C is correct because the legal team's argument hinges precisely on the company's prior knowledge of the specific vulnerability, which defeats any claim that the harm was unforeseeable; A is wrong because the argument does not establish liability for any defect but specifically for one the company already knew about; B is wrong because the legal team's reasoning is about liability once harm occurs, not about what release policy companies should follow; D is wrong because the legal team's argument concerns the company's own liability and says nothing about the relative culpability of the hackers; E is wrong because the legal team does not argue that deadline-driven releases are never justified, only that prior knowledge of a specific risk forecloses the unforeseeability defense.

stimulus: Principle: A city government is obligated to compensate a business owner for lost revenue when the city undertakes a construction project that blocks pedestrian access to that business for more than thirty consecutive days, provided that the business was operating profitably in the twelve months prior to the start of construction.
stem: Which one of the following judgments most closely conforms to the principle stated above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Marino's Bakery is owed compensation from the city because a water main repair project blocked the bakery's entrance for forty days, even though the bakery had been operating at a loss for the past two years.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The city is obligated to compensate Hargrove Electronics because a road-widening project has blocked vehicle access to the store's parking lot for three weeks, and the store was profitable throughout last year.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patel's Restaurant is owed compensation from the city because a bridge renovation blocked pedestrian access to the restaurant for thirty-five days, and the restaurant had been profitable for the eighteen months before construction began.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The city must compensate Reyes Florist because a sidewalk repair project lasting fifty days reduced foot traffic to the florist's shop, even though the florist had only opened six months before construction started and had yet to turn a profit.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Chung's Bookstore deserves compensation from the city because the store lost significant revenue during a utility installation project, despite the fact that pedestrians could still reach the store's entrance throughout the project.

explanation: C is correct because it satisfies all conditions of the principle: a city construction project blocked pedestrian access for more than thirty consecutive days (thirty-five days) and the business was operating profitably in the twelve months prior to construction; A fails because the business was not profitable in the prior twelve months; B fails because the obstruction blocked vehicle access, not pedestrian access; D fails because the business had not been operating profitably in the twelve months prior to construction; and E fails because pedestrian access was not actually blocked.

stimulus: Principle: A physician who prescribes a medication to a patient is acting negligently if the physician failed to review the patient's complete medication list before prescribing and the prescribed medication has a known harmful interaction with any medication the patient was already taking.
stem: Which one of the following judgments most closely conforms to the principle stated above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dr. Okafor acted negligently when she prescribed a blood thinner to Hendricks, since Hendricks suffered a severe adverse reaction to the medication, even though Dr. Okafor had reviewed Hendricks's complete medication list beforehand.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dr. Okafor acted negligently when she prescribed an antibiotic to Hendricks without reviewing his medication list, even though the antibiotic has no known harmful interactions with any other medications.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dr. Okafor acted negligently when she prescribed a cholesterol-lowering drug to Hendricks, since she failed to review his complete medication list before prescribing, and the drug she prescribed is known to interact harmfully with the blood pressure medication Hendricks was already taking.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dr. Okafor acted negligently when she prescribed a pain reliever to Hendricks, since the pain reliever has a known harmful interaction with a sedative that Hendricks occasionally uses recreationally, even though Dr. Okafor had carefully reviewed his complete medication list.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Dr. Okafor acted negligently when she prescribed an antidepressant to Hendricks without reviewing his complete medication list, even though Hendricks was not taking any other medications at the time of the prescription.

explanation: C is correct because it satisfies both conditions of the principle: Dr. Okafor failed to review the complete medication list AND the prescribed drug has a known harmful interaction with a medication the patient was already taking; A is wrong because Dr. Okafor did review the medication list, so the first condition is not met; B is wrong because the antibiotic has no known harmful interactions, so the second condition is not met; D is wrong because Dr. Okafor did review the medication list, so the first condition fails; E is wrong because Hendricks was not taking any other medications, so there can be no harmful interaction with an existing medication, failing the second condition.
